title: Logarithmic Entanglement and Emergent Dipole Symmetry from a Strongly Coupled Light-Matter Quantum Circuit
summary: Hybrid systems where a quantum material strongly couples to a nonlocal cavity photon mode have emerged as a new frontier for controlling and probing quantum correlations, yet the structure and scaling of light-matter entanglement produced by the nonlocal coupling remains poorly understood. We address this problem through an exactly solvable framework based on reinterpreting the Power--Zienau--Woolley (PZW) transformation as a \textit{light-matter quantum circuit} that couples the photonic position quadrature $X \sim a + a^\dagger$ to the many-body dipole $\mathcal{P}$ of a one-dimensional quantum chain. We derive a closed-form expression for the reduced density matrix valid at all coupling strengths, in which off-diagonal elements between matter states of unequal dipole are suppressed by a Gaussian factor encoding the full weak-to-ultrastrong coupling crossover. At weak coupling, the reduced density matrix takes a Lindbladian form with $\mathcal{P}$ as the jump operator, and the entanglement entropy is controlled by the dipole variance. At ultrastrong coupling, the density matrix becomes exactly block-diagonal in dipole sectors, reflecting an \textit{emergent dipole symmetry} dynamically imposed by the photon field, with entanglement entropy given exactly by the Shannon entropy of the dipole-sector weight distribution. Applying this framework to a half-filled Su--Schrieffer--Heeger chain, we show that, at strong coupling, both the light-matter entanglement and the spatial entanglement of the photon-dressed matter state scale logarithmically with system size, $S_\infty \sim \fracα{2}\log L$, robust across the SSH phase diagram. The logarithm originates from the photon resolving a single collective coordinate $\mathcal{P}$ whose fluctuations grow as $L^{α/2}$, a distinct mechanism from the logarithmic entanglement of critical one-dimensional systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an exactly solvable framework using the Power-Zienau-Woolley transformation to study light-matter entanglement in hybrid quantum systems coupling nonlocal cavity photons to a one-dimensional quantum chain. The researchers derive a closed-form reduced density matrix covering weak-to-ultrastrong coupling regimes, discovering that entanglement entropy scales logarithmically with system size due to the photon resolving a single collective dipole coordinate.
AI.motivation: Hybrid systems where quantum materials strongly couple to nonlocal cavity photons have emerged as a new frontier for controlling quantum correlations, but the structure and scaling of light-matter entanglement produced by nonlocal coupling remains poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors develop an exactly solvable framework by reinterpreting the Power-Zienau-Woolley (PZW) transformation as a light-matter quantum circuit that couples the photonic position quadrature to the many-body dipole of a one-dimensional quantum chain. They derive a closed-form expression for the reduced density matrix valid at all coupling strengths.
AI.result: At weak coupling, the reduced density matrix takes a Lindbladian form with the dipole as the jump operator, and entanglement entropy is controlled by dipole variance. At ultrastrong coupling, the density matrix becomes block-diagonal in dipole sectors due to emergent dipole symmetry, with entanglement entropy given by the Shannon entropy of dipole-sector weight distribution. For a half-filled Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain at strong coupling, both light-matter entanglement and spatial entanglement of photon-dressed matter state scale logarithmically with system size.
AI.conclusion: The logarithmic scaling S∞ ∼ α/2 log L originates from the photon resolving a single collective coordinate P whose fluctuations grow as L^α/2, providing a distinct mechanism from the logarithmic entanglement of critical one-dimensional systems. This finding is robust across the SSH phase diagram.

title: Hatsugai-Kohmoto-like Models for Altermagnets and Odd-Parity Magnets
summary: We introduce a generalized Hatsugai-Kohmoto multi-orbital model and study its phase diagram and physical properties in the additional presence of perturbations that lift any extensive ground-state degeneracies. The unperturbed, exactly solvable model already displays a rich set of spectral functions, including regimes reminiscent of unconventional magnets. We map the first-order study of additional spatially local multi-orbital Hubbard interactions to a Heisenberg model in momentum space, which leads to symmetry-breaking instabilities already at weak coupling. Interestingly, translational-symmetry breaking orders, such as antiferromagnetism, are excluded. Instead, in addition to ferromagnetism, unconventional $p$-wave and $d$-wave magnets occur, characterized by spin order on the bonds of the underlying square lattice. Adding another type of momentum-space interaction, which still allows to solve the model exactly, is shown to stabilize a non-degenerate singlet ground state that retains the spin splitting characteristic of unconventional magnets. We discuss its impact on the spin structure factor. Taken together, our findings show that Hatsugai-Kohmoto-like models provide a rich playground for unconventional magnetism.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a generalized Hatsugai-Kohmoto multi-orbital model and investigates its phase diagram, discovering unconventional p-wave and d-wave magnetic orders alongside ferromagnetism, while excluding antiferromagnetism. The work demonstrates these models provide a rich framework for studying unconventional magnetism.
AI.motivation: This research addresses the need to understand unconventional magnetic states in multi-orbital systems and explore exactly solvable models that can capture complex magnetic phenomena beyond conventional magnetism.
AI.method: The study employs a generalized Hatsugai-Kohmoto multi-orbital model, mapping multi-orbital Hubbard interactions to a Heisenberg model in momentum space to analyze symmetry-breaking instabilities at weak coupling. Additional momentum-space interactions are introduced to stabilize the ground state.
AI.result: The model exhibits ferromagnetic and unconventional p-wave and d-wave magnets characterized by spin order on lattice bonds, while translational-symmetry breaking orders like antiferromagnetism are excluded. A non-degenerate singlet ground state retaining spin splitting from unconventional magnets can be stabilized.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that Hatsugai-Kohmoto-like models serve as a rich platform for exploring unconventional magnetism, with the model supporting ferromagnetic and unconventional p-wave/d-wave magnetic orders while excluding conventional antiferromagnetism. The work opens avenues for studying quantum magnetism in these exactly solvable multi-orbital systems.

title: Exploring Entropic Orders: High Temperature Continuous Symmetry Breaking, Chiral Topological States and Local Commuting Projector Models
summary: High temperature is usually expected to destroy order: as the Gibbs state approaches the infinite-temperature limit, it becomes an equal-weight ensemble over all states and the system is generically disordered. Recent works showed that entropic order can violate this expectation through coupling to bosons in classical lattice models and quantum field theories, where the ordered states have higher entropy. Here we present new analytic methods for constructing quantum lattice models that exhibit entropic orders. In particular, we construct quantum lattice models with continuous symmetry breaking at high temperature in 1+1 dimensions and clarify how entropic order can evade the Hohenberg-Mermin-Wagner theorems. We also construct high-temperature entropic $p+ip$ chiral topological superconducting states in 2+1 dimensions with temperature-independent anyon correlation functions. In addition, we obtain a broad family of high-temperature entropic non-chiral topological orders. We show that the entropic topological orders have strong higher form symmetries at high temperature unlike the conventional topological orders, and the symmetry is spontaneously broken. These results follow from two general constructions that couple a given lattice model with a low-temperature ordered phase either to ordered bosons or, for local commuting-projector Hamiltonians, to more general bosonic degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: This paper presents new analytic methods for constructing quantum lattice models exhibiting entropic order, where ordered states have higher entropy than disordered states at high temperatures, contrary to typical thermodynamic expectations.
AI.motivation: High temperature typically destroys order, with Gibbs states approaching infinite-temperature limits as equal-weight ensembles. However, recent works showed entropic order can violate this expectation through coupling to bosons in classical lattice models and quantum field theories. This work aims to construct quantum lattice models exhibiting entropic orders and clarify theoretical foundations.
AI.method: The authors develop two general construction methods: (1) coupling a lattice model with low-temperature ordered phase to ordered bosons, and (2) for local commuting-projector Hamiltonians, coupling to more general bosonic degrees of freedom. They construct 1+1D models with continuous symmetry breaking at high temperature that evade Hohenberg-Mermin-Wagner theorems, and 2+1D high-temperature entropic p+ip chiral topological superconducting states with temperature-independent anyon correlation functions.
AI.result: The work obtains: (1) quantum lattice models with continuous symmetry breaking at high temperature in 1+1D that evade Hohenberg-Mermin-Wagner theorems, (2) high-temperature entropic p+ip chiral topological superconducting states in 2+1D with temperature-independent anyon correlation functions, and (3) a broad family of high-temperature entropic non-chiral topological orders. Entropic topological orders exhibit strong higher form symmetries at high temperature, unlike conventional topological orders, with spontaneous symmetry breaking.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that entropic order can exist in quantum lattice models across multiple dimensions, establishing theoretical foundations for understanding how ordered phases can persist or emerge at high temperatures through entropic mechanisms. The findings challenge conventional understanding of temperature-order relationships in many-body physics and provide new avenues for exploring topological phases.

title: Conformal Data for the $O(2)$ Wilson-Fisher CFT in $(2+1)$-Dimensional Spacetime from Exact Diagonalization and Matrix Product States on the Fuzzy Sphere
summary: We study at zero temperature a microscopic quantum spin-1 model on the fuzzy sphere that realizes the $O(2)$ Wilson-Fisher conformal field theory (CFT) in $(2+1)$-dimensional spacetime at a quantum critical point. Here, we use the fuzzy-sphere regularization as it preserves the full spatial $SO(3)$ rotational symmetry of the CFT, enabling the state-operator correspondence that maps energy eigenstates directly to CFT operators. Using exact diagonalization (ED) and matrix product state (MPS) techniques combined with conformal perturbation theory (CPT), we extract conformal data including scaling dimensions and operator product expansion (OPE) coefficients. We identify 32 primary operators and their descendants, organized by the conserved $O(2)$ charge $S^{z}$ and spatial angular momentum $L$. Our numerical results for the scaling dimensions of the lowest primary operators show good agreement with conformal bootstrap predictions. We verify predictions from the large charge expansion, which provides systematic predictions for operators carrying large $U(1)$ charge, connecting the Goldstone mode physics in the ordered phase to phonon primaries at the critical point.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a quantum spin-1 model on the fuzzy sphere realizing O(2) Wilson-Fisher CFT in (2+1)D, using ED and MPS combined with CPT to extract conformal data. 32 primary operators were identified with scaling dimensions matching conformal bootstrap predictions, verifying large charge expansion theory.
AI.motivation: The O(2) Wilson-Fisher CFT in (2+1) dimensions is a fundamental conformal field theory describing quantum criticality, but studying it with traditional lattice regularization suffers from rotational symmetry breaking. The fuzzy sphere approach preserves the full spatial SO(3) symmetry, enabling the crucial state-operator correspondence that directly maps energy eigenstates to CFT operators - a key advantage over other regularization schemes.
AI.method: The researchers employed a multi-faceted numerical approach: (1) Fuzzy-sphere regularization to maintain full SO(3) rotational symmetry, (2) Exact diagonalization (ED) for small system sizes, (3) Matrix product state (MPS) techniques for larger systems, and (4) Conformal perturbation theory (CPT) to extract scaling dimensions and OPE coefficients from numerical data.
AI.result: The study identified 32 primary operators and their descendants, organized by O(2) charge (Sz) and spatial angular momentum (L). Numerical scaling dimensions of lowest primary operators show good agreement with conformal bootstrap predictions. The large charge expansion predictions were verified, demonstrating connections between Goldstone mode physics in the ordered phase and phonon primaries at the critical point.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates that the fuzzy sphere regularization is an effective framework for studying O(2) Wilson-Fisher CFT in (2+1)D, preserving spatial symmetries that enable direct mapping between energy eigenstates and CFT operators. The numerical results strongly corroborate conformal bootstrap predictions and verify large charge expansion theory, bridging Goldstone mode physics with phonon primaries at the critical point.

title: Competition and coexistence of superconductivity and nematic order in a two-dimensional electron gas with quadrupolar interactions
summary: We investigate the interplay between superconductivity and nematic order in a two-dimensional electron gas with competing pairing and quadrupolar forward-scattering interactions. The model includes both $s$-wave and $d$-wave superconducting channels. We compute the mean-field free energy density and determine the phase diagrams as functions of interaction strengths and temperature by solving a set of coupled self-consistent equations. At zero-temperature, we find that the nematic order competes strongly with $d$-wave superconductivity, leading to a direct first-order phase transition, while its interplay with $s$-wave pairing allows for a coexistence phase characterized by an anisotropic Fermi surface with a uniform superconducting gap. At finite-temperatures, quadrupolar interactions promote the emergence of additional superconducting components, giving rise to regimes where $s$-wave, $d$-wave, and nematic orders coexist. Our results highlight the role of symmetry and interaction strength in shaping the phase structure and provide a minimal framework to describe intertwined nematic and superconducting phases in correlated electron systems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the competition and coexistence between nematic order and superconductivity (both s-wave and d-wave channels) in a 2D electron gas using mean-field theory. Key findings include first-order transition between nematic and d-wave states at T=0, but coexistence possible with s-wave pairing, while finite temperatures allow triple coexistence of all three orders.
AI.motivation: The study investigates the interplay between superconductivity and nematic order in a two-dimensional electron gas with competing pairing and quadrupolar forward-scattering interactions.
AI.method: The authors compute the mean-field free energy density and determine the phase diagrams as functions of interaction strengths and temperature by solving a set of coupled self-consistent equations.
AI.result: At zero-temperature, the nematic order competes strongly with d-wave superconductivity, leading to a direct first-order phase transition, while its interplay with s-wave pairing allows for a coexistence phase characterized by an anisotropic Fermi surface with a uniform superconducting gap. At finite-temperatures, quadrupolar interactions promote the emergence of additional superconducting components, giving rise to regimes where s-wave, d-wave, and nematic orders coexist.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the role of symmetry and interaction strength in shaping the phase structure and provide a minimal framework to describe intertwined nematic and superconducting phases in correlated electron systems.

title: Dynamical magnetism in the disordered cubic lattice material $γ$-${\rm Ba}_{3}{\rm CoNb}_{2}{\rm O}_{9}$
summary: $γ$-${\rm Ba}_{3}{\rm CoNb}_{2}{\rm O}_{9}$ realizes a disordered simple-cubic spin-$1/2$ lattice in which Co$^{2+}$ ions randomly occupy one third of the sites, placing the system close to the site-percolation threshold for magnetic order. Specific-heat, susceptibility, neutron spin-echo, and muon spin-rotation measurements reveal a broad thermodynamic crossover, short-range magnetic correlations, and persistent fast spin dynamics down to at least 0.1~K, with no evidence for static order or conventional spin-glass freezing. Monte Carlo simulations yield a broad distribution of orphan spins, finite clusters, and an infinite network. The calculated orphan-spin fraction ($\approx 8.8\%$) agrees well with the weakly correlated spin fraction inferred from magnetization ($\approx 8.2\%$). Exact diagonalization of a diluted $S = 1/2$ Heisenberg model captures the broad magnetic specific-heat anomaly and supports the coexistence of weakly and strongly correlated spin environments. These results support a picture in which spin-$1/2$ quantum fluctuations, together with dilution and proximity to the percolation threshold, can support a disorder-driven dynamical state with short-range correlations in three dimensions, distinct from both classical spin glasses and geometrically frustrated quantum spin liquids.

AI.tldr: A disordered spin-1/2 system (γ-Ba3CoNb2O9) close to the percolation threshold exhibits no static order or spin-glass freezing, but shows broad thermodynamic crossover, short-range correlations, and persistent fast quantum spin dynamics down to 0.1 K.
AI.motivation: The compound provides a nearly percolating, disordered simple-cubic lattice of spin-1/2 Co2+ ions, offering a chance to explore how site dilution and proximity to the percolation threshold influence magnetic order and quantum fluctuations in three dimensions.
AI.method: Specific-heat, susceptibility, neutron spin-echo, muon spin-rotation, magnetization measurements combined with Monte Carlo simulations and exact diagonalization of a diluted S=1/2 Heisenberg model.
AI.result: 发现了广泛的磁热交叉、孤子自旋的有限分数(≈8.8%与≈8.2%对应)以及短程关联，但没有长程有序或传统自旋玻璃冻结。模拟再现了实验的热异常，并支持弱关联与强关联自旋环境的共存。
AI.conclusion: Disorder-driven dynamical state in three dimensions emerges from spin‑½ quantum fluctuations together with dilution and proximity to percolation, distinct from classical spin glasses and geometrically frustrated quantum spin liquids.

title: General Conditions for Axis Dependent Conduction Polarity
summary: Axis-Dependent Conduction Polarity (ADCP) refers to the phenomenon in which electrical transport within a single material is p-type along one crystallographic direction and n-type along the perpendicular direction. This behavior enables a variety of thermoelectric applications that do not require a heterojunction between two different materials. In this work, we investigate ADCP theoretically and derive a set of generic and quantitative criteria for identifying and predicting materials that exhibit ADCP. Specifically, by analyzing the thermopower for generic metals, semimetals, and semiconductors, we obtain transparent inequalities that are both necessary and sufficient for the emergence of ADCP. Moreover, we review known ADCP materials and verify that their band-structure characteristics and relaxation parameters are consistent with the inequalities derived here.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes quantitative criteria for identifying materials exhibiting axis-dependent conduction polarity, where a single material shows p-type conduction along one crystallographic direction and n-type along another, enabling simplified thermoelectric device design without heterojunctions.
AI.motivation: Thermoelectric devices typically require p-n heterojunctions between different materials. A phenomenon enabling single-material polarity control could simplify device fabrication and expand design flexibility for thermoelectric applications.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation from thermopower analysis for generic metals, semimetals, and semiconductors, combined with band-structure and relaxation parameter analysis of known materials.
AI.result: Obtained transparent, quantitative inequalities that are both necessary and sufficient for ADCP emergence. Verified consistency of band-structure characteristics and relaxation parameters in known ADCP materials with the derived criteria.
AI.conclusion: The proposed inequalities provide a practical screening tool for discovering new ADCP materials, opening avenues for designing heterojunction-free thermoelectric devices with direction-tunable polarity.

title: Stripping Symmetry: Electrochemical Oxidation to a Superconducting Polar Metal in Au2Pb0.914P2
comment: 105 pages, 5 main figures, 41 supplemental figures, 19 supplemental tables
summary: Polar metals and noncentrosymmetric superconductors are exceptionally rare, yet their broken inversion symmetry can give rise to emergent electronic phenomena including mixed singlet-triplet superconducting pairing. As only a few such materials have been found among known compounds, accessing new examples requires synthetic strategies that go beyond conventional crystal growth. Here, we use electrochemical topotactic deintercalation to remove Pb from the centrosymmetric parent compound Au$_2$PbP$_2$, producing the polar metal Au$_2$Pb$_{0.914}$P$_2$. Unlike conventional chemical doping, this transformation actively drives structural symmetry-breaking: the partial removal of Pb triggers a cooperative electronic and geometric rearrangement, mediated by a second-order Jahn-Teller effect and stereochemically active lone pairs, that locks the product into a polar, noncentrosymmetric superspace group Ama2(01g)ss0. We solve the complete (3+1)D modulated structure by synchrotron single-crystal X-ray diffraction and confirm the polar assignment through nonlinear electronic transport. Below T$_c$ = 1.52 K, Au$_2$Pb$_{0.914}$P$_2$ becomes a type-II superconductor whose heat capacity and AC susceptibility both exhibit power-law behavior, suggestive of a gap structure governed by the broken inversion symmetry of the host lattice. This work establishes electrochemical oxidation as a rational route to metastable noncentrosymmetric superconductors through chemically directed symmetry-breaking.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that electrochemical topotactic deintercalation can transform a centrosymmetric compound (Au₂PbP₂) into a polar, noncentrosymmetric superconductor (Au₂Pb₀.914P₂) with Tc = 1.52 K, establishing a new synthetic strategy for creating metastable noncentrosymmetric superconductors through chemically-driven symmetry-breaking.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of polar metals and noncentrosymmetric superconductors among known compounds limits the study of emergent electronic phenomena like mixed singlet-triplet superconducting pairing. Conventional crystal growth methods have yielded only a few examples, necessitating new synthetic strategies beyond traditional approaches.
AI.method: Electrochemical topotactic deintercalation was used to remove Pb from the centrosymmetric parent compound Au₂PbP₂. This technique actively drives structural symmetry-breaking through partial Pb removal, triggering a cooperative electronic and geometric rearrangement mediated by a second-order Jahn-Teller effect and stereochemically active lone pairs.
AI.result: The produced material Au₂Pb₀.914P₂ crystallizes in a polar, noncentrosymmetric superspace group Ama2(01g)ss0. It becomes a type-II superconductor below Tc = 1.52 K, with heat capacity and AC susceptibility exhibiting power-law behavior suggestive of gap structure governed by broken inversion symmetry.
AI.conclusion: Electrochemical oxidation is established as a rational synthetic route to metastable noncentrosymmetric superconductors through chemically directed symmetry-breaking, providing a new pathway to access materials with exotic electronic properties.

title: Room-temperature multistage metastability in a moiré superstructure
summary: Metastability is fundamental not only to phase ordering and transitions, but also to a broad range of modern technologies, from memory devices to metallic glasses. In condensed-matter physics, charge density waves (CDWs) offer versatile platforms for accessing metastable states due to their sensitivity to external stimuli. However, most metastable CDW states are stabilized only at low temperatures, limiting their practical utility. In this study, we report the observation of electrically driven, room-temperature, nonvolatile metastable states in the bulk form of EuTe$_4$, a recently discovered compound that hosts an innate moiré superlattice characterized by the stacking of incommensurate monolayer and bilayer CDWs. Systematic transport measurements reveal discrete resistivity plateaus and strong electric-field sensitivity, with a large number of metastable states readily induced across a wide temperature window within a giant hysteresis loop, making them well-suited for high-temperature, multi-bit memory applications. By integrating photoemission spectroscopy, diffraction, and in-situ transport measurements, we uncover that these metastable states do not stem from conventional mechanisms such as the emergence of new ordered phases or changes in incommensurate periodicity. Instead, they are characterized by a suppression of the original CDW amplitude and a reduction in correlation length, pointing to a unique electric-field-induced switching of out-of-plane CDW phases in the moiré superstructure. Our findings not only provide critical insights into metastable phenomena in moiré systems with stacked electronic orders but also establish EuTe$_4$ as a promising platform for developing room-temperature, multi-bit memory devices.

AI.tldr: This study reports electrically driven, nonvolatile metastable states at room temperature in EuTe₄, a compound with an innate moiré superlattice. These metastable states show discrete resistivity plateaus and are useful for high-temperature, multi-bit memory applications. The metastable states result from suppression of CDW amplitude and reduction in correlation length, rather than new ordered phases.
AI.motivation: Metastable states in condensed-matter physics are important but typically limited to low temperatures. The researchers aimed to find metastable charge density wave (CDW) states that remain stable at room temperature for practical applications in memory devices.
AI.method: Systematic transport measurements were combined with photoemission spectroscopy and diffraction, along with in-situ transport measurements to characterize the metastable states.
AI.result: Discovery of electrically driven, room-temperature, nonvolatile metastable states in bulk EuTe₄. These states show discrete resistivity plateaus with strong electric-field sensitivity across a wide temperature window within a giant hysteresis loop. The metastable states are characterized by suppression of original CDW amplitude and reduction in correlation length, pointing to electric-field-induced switching of out-of-plane CDW phases in the moiré superstructure.
AI.conclusion: EuTe₄ is a promising platform for developing room-temperature, multi-bit memory devices. The findings provide insights into metastable phenomena in moiré systems with stacked electronic orders.

title: Proximity Magnetism in Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4-(Bi,Sb)2Te3/MnTe Natural Heterostructures
summary: Magnetic topological insulators and their heterostructures provide great opportunities in coupling band topology with nontrivial spin configuration for enhanced spintronic device performance as well as designing totally new magnetoelectric systems and functionalities. We find that Mn interdiffusion from MnTe when interfaced with (Bi,Sb)2Te3 stabilizes as self-organized Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4 septuple lamellae amongst alternating (Bi,Sb)2Te3 quintuple layers, as observed using scanning transmission electron microscopy and depth-sensitive polarized neutron reflectometry. We further demonstrate a valuable combination of magnetic and topological orders in these naturally formed Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4-(Bi,Sb)2Te3 heterostructures that are exchange coupled with MnTe. Magnetotransport experiments and quantum magnetism simulations reveal that, above its own Neel temperature TN of 20 K, Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4 mediates the exchange field leading to an anomalous Hall effect at the (Bi,Sb)2Te3/MnTe interface, with an enhanced interfacial TN exceeding 200 K. This novel magnetic interface in turn allows a robust and deterministic spin-orbit torque switching without an external magnetic field at a low critical current density of 300 kA cm-2. The antiferromagnetically coupled architecture of Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4-(Bi,Sb)2Te3/MnTe, featuring unique magnetic and topological proximity effects across a chalcogenide backbone, is rich in fundamental interface physics and holds potential for practical applications in spintronics.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates magnetic topological insulators and their heterostructures, demonstrating that Mn interdiffusion creates self-organized Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4 septuple lamellae within (Bi,Sb)2Te3 layers, leading to exchange-coupled heterostructures with enhanced interfacial Neel temperature exceeding 200 K and enabling robust spin-orbit torque switching at low current densities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the coupling of band topology with nontrivial spin configuration in magnetic topological insulators for enhanced spintronic device performance and to design new magnetoelectric systems with combined magnetic and topological orders.
AI.method: The methods include scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) for structural observation, depth-sensitive polarized neutron reflectometry (PNR) for depth-sensitive analysis, magnetotransport experiments for measuring electrical properties, and quantum magnetism simulations for theoretical understanding.
AI.result: The key results show that Mn interdiffusion stabilizes Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4 septuple lamellae amongst alternating (Bi,Sb)2Te3 quintuple layers. These heterostructures exhibit exchange coupling with MnTe, leading to an anomalous Hall effect at the (Bi,Sb)2Te3/MnTe interface with interfacial TN exceeding 200 K. This enables deterministic spin-orbit torque switching without external magnetic field at a low critical current density of 300 kA cm-2.
AI.conclusion: The antiferromagnetically coupled architecture of Mn(Bi,Sb)2Te4-(Bi,Sb)2Te3/MnTe with unique magnetic and topological proximity effects across a chalcogenide backbone provides rich fundamental interface physics and holds potential for practical applications in spintronic devices.

title: AngstromPro: A versatile software for massive N-dimensional STM data management, visualization and in-depth analysis
comment: The source code for this project is open source and available on Github at https://github.com/jhyan2018/ AngstromPro
summary: We present AngstromPro, a versatile, modular and open-source software built on Python for managing, visualizing and analyzing large datasets acquired via Scanning Tunneling Microscopes (STM). Its robust architecture features a top-level module that manages a Global Variables List and a sub-modules List. Each sub-module, equipped with its own Local Variables List to maintain a tidy workspace, can be tailored for specific tasks, including built-in modules like the Multiple 2D Images Visualizer and Analyzer. These modules support step-by-step data processing and extensibility for custom algorithms and functions. AngstromPro's design supports a wide range of users, from those relying on built-in tools to developers creating custom algorithms or extending the platform with new modules. In its implementation, AngstromPro separates graphical user interface components from data processing algorithms, a strategy that enhances code readability, maintainability, and extensibility. The embedded algorithms reflect commonly adopted and recently developed approaches in STM data analysis, including background subtraction, perfect lattice correction, Lawler-Fujita correction, and sub-atomic precision registration, along with additional standard data processing routines. By consolidating fragmented STM workflows, maintaining detailed processing histories, and providing a flexible platform for customization, AngstromPro enhances both the efficiency, reliability, and reproducibility of STM data analysis, while enabling the rapid development of new methods and modules.

AI.tldr: AngstromPro is an open-source Python software for Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) data management, visualization, and analysis. It features a modular architecture with built-in tools and extensibility for custom algorithms, addressing fragmented STM workflows while enhancing efficiency, reliability, and reproducibility of STM data analysis.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind AngstromPro stems from the need to address fragmented STM (Scanning Tunneling Microscope) workflows in the scientific community. STM data analysis lacks unified, comprehensive software solutions, leading to inefficient, less reproducible research. The field needed a versatile, modular platform that could handle large datasets while maintaining detailed processing histories and supporting both built-in tools and custom algorithm development.
AI.method: AngstromPro employs a modular architecture with a top-level module managing a Global Variables List and sub-modules each with their own Local Variables List. The design separates graphical user interface components from data processing algorithms to enhance code readability, maintainability, and extensibility. Built-in modules include the Multiple 2D Images Visualizer and Analyzer supporting step-by-step data processing. Embedded algorithms include background subtraction, perfect lattice correction, Lawler-Fujita correction, sub-atomic precision registration, and standard data processing routines.
AI.result: The result is a versatile, modular, open-source Python software called AngstromPro that successfully manages, visualizes, and analyzes large STM datasets. The platform provides built-in modules for visualization and analysis, incorporates commonly used and recently developed STM data analysis approaches, maintains detailed processing histories, and offers a flexible platform for customization and extension with new modules.
AI.conclusion: AngstromPro successfully consolidates fragmented STM workflows into a unified, extensible platform. The software addresses critical gaps in STM data analysis by providing comprehensive tools while maintaining flexibility for custom development. Its separation of GUI and processing algorithms enhances maintainability, and the detailed processing histories improve reproducibility. The availability of both built-in tools and customization options makes it accessible to a wide range of users, from novice STM users to advanced developers creating custom algorithms.

title: Energy relaxation due to two-phonon scattering of electrons: Breakdown of the energy diffusion model
summary: Recent THz spectroscopy of the quantum paraelectric SrTiO$_3$ (arXiv:2501.15771) and a high-$T_c$ cuprate (arXiv:2503.15646) has renewed interest in energy relaxation in correlated electron systems. We consider a situation in which single-phonon scattering is forbidden by symmetry or momentum conservation, while two-phonon scattering is allowed. Solving the Boltzmann equation, we show that above the Bloch-Grüneisen temperature the energy relaxation rate from two soft transverse optical phonons exceeds the single-phonon one: while the latter scales as $1/T$, the former is linear in $T$. This dominance of two-phonon scattering invalidates the usual picture of energy diffusion due to frequent scattering by subthermal phonons; instead, energy relaxes via rare scattering events involving thermal phonons. Below the Bloch-Grüneisen temperature, the energy relaxation rate scales as the single-particle rate, namely as $T^3$ for soft phonons. For anisotropic electron bands, an intermediate regime appears between two Bloch-Grüneisen temperatures, in which both allowed single-phonon and two-phonon processes scale as $T^2$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies energy relaxation in correlated electron systems where single-phonon scattering is forbidden. Using Boltzmann equation analysis, the authors show that above the Bloch-Grüneisen temperature, two-phonon scattering (scaling as T) dominates over single-phonon scattering (scaling as 1/T), fundamentally changing the energy relaxation mechanism from diffusion of subthermal phonons to rare thermal phonon events.
AI.motivation: Recent THz spectroscopy experiments on quantum paraelectric SrTiO₃ and high-Tc cuprates have renewed interest in energy relaxation mechanisms in correlated electron systems where single-phonon scattering is forbidden by symmetry or momentum conservation
AI.method: Solving the Boltzmann equation to calculate energy relaxation rates from two soft transverse optical phonons, comparing single-phonon vs two-phonon scattering mechanisms across different temperature regimes
AI.result: Above the Bloch-Grüneisen temperature, two-phonon scattering dominates over single-phonon scattering: while single-phonon rate scales as 1/T, two-phonon rate is linear in T. Below the Bloch-Grüneisen temperature, the energy relaxation rate scales as T³ for soft phonons. Anisotropic electron bands show an intermediate regime with T² scaling.
AI.conclusion: The dominance of two-phonon scattering at high temperatures invalidates the usual picture of energy diffusion via frequent subthermal phonon scattering; instead, energy relaxes through rare thermal phonon events. For anisotropic electron bands, an intermediate regime exists between two Bloch-Grüneisen temperatures where both single-phonon and two-phonon processes scale as T².

title: Magnetic Polarons Enable Exceptional Magnetocaloric Response
summary: Magnetocaloric materials are typically limited by a trade-off between magnetic entropy and field responsiveness. Here we show that magnetic polarons provide an intermediate regime that mitigates this constraint and enables an exceptional magnetocaloric response. Using EuB$_6$ as a model system, we combine thermodynamic and magnetic measurements to demonstrate that nanoscale ferromagnetic clusters emerging near the Curie temperature strongly enhance the field-induced entropy collapse. These clusters possess large effective moments that respond efficiently to applied fields while retaining substantial entropy due to their small size and dynamic fluctuations. As a result, EuB$_6$ exhibits a giant cryogenic magnetocaloric response, with both large isothermal entropy change and adiabatic temperature change in the technologically important 10-40 K range. Our results identify magnetic polarons as an underexplored route for optimizing magnetocaloric performance and establish an intermediate magnetic length scale as a design principle for high-performance cryogenic cooling materials.

title: Pseudogap and Condensation in Cuprate Superconductors from NMR Shifts
summary: The electronic properties of the high-temperature superconducting cuprates are encoded in complex sets of NMR data, but without microscopic theory, reliable NMR phenomenologies are in demand. Early analyses of NMR could only focus on very few materials and discovered spin singlet pairing and the enigmatic pseudogap. However, a coherent phenomenology of shift and relaxation could not be established, as incoming data from other cuprates complicated the picture. Today, due to work of many groups worldwide, planar copper and oxygen NMR data are available for most cuprates. Here, based only on symmetry of the two Cu hyperfine couplings, an anisotropic $A_α$ and isotropic $B$, the Cu shifts are disentangled, and two different shift components emerge. Upon doping the cuprates, metallic B-spins are created above the pseudogap temperature which is shared with metallic A-spins. Further doping decreases the pseudogap temperature and increases the B-spin, but less so the A-spin. The apparent linear rate of increase in density of states of the B-spin with doping increases nearly threefold above about $x=0.20$, where the pseudogap has disappeared and A and B turn into superconducting metals, i.e. they disappear rapidly at $T_\mathrm{c}$. The pseudogap temperature is a measure of the coupling between A and B, which suppresses the shifts but not nuclear relaxation. Spin singlet pairing involves A and B according to three simple rules for condensation which will be discussed. The optimal $T_\mathrm{c}$ demands a special match between A and B and involves systems with a pseudogap. However, the highest $T_\mathrm{c}$ of all cuprates is not encoded in the shift, but rather in nuclear relaxation and charge sharing between planar Cu and O. Relations to other probes are discussed.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a coherent NMR phenomenology for high-temperature superconducting cuprates by using symmetry analysis of Cu hyperfine couplings. Two distinct spin components (A and B) are identified, and their behavior with doping reveals the pseudogap's origin, spin singlet pairing mechanisms, and conditions for optimal Tc.
AI.motivation: The electronic properties of high-Tc superconducting cuprates are encoded in complex NMR data, but without microscopic theory, reliable NMR phenomenologies are lacking. Early NMR analyses could only study few materials and discovered spin singlet pairing and pseudogap, but couldn't establish coherent phenomenology due to complications from data across different cuprates.
AI.method: Based only on symmetry of the two Cu hyperfine couplings - anisotropic A_α and isotropic B - the Cu shifts are disentangled, revealing two different shift components (A-spins and B-spins). This symmetry-based approach allows analysis of planar copper and oxygen NMR data across most cuprates.
AI.result: Upon doping cuprates, metallic B-spins are created above the pseudogap temperature (shared with metallic A-spins). Further doping decreases pseudogap temperature and increases B-spin (less so A-spin). The linear rate of increase in B-spin density of states nearly triples above x=0.20, where pseudogap disappears and A+B become superconducting metals (disappear rapidly at Tc). Pseudogap temperature measures coupling between A and B, suppressing shifts but not nuclear relaxation. Spin singlet pairing involves A and B according to three condensation rules. Optimal Tc requires special A-B match and systems with pseudogap. Highest Tc is encoded in nuclear relaxation and charge sharing between planar Cu and O.
AI.conclusion: The NMR phenomenology successfully establishes relationships between A-spins, B-spins, pseudogap, and superconductivity. The pseudogap arises from A-B coupling. Optimal Tc conditions involve specific A-B matching and presence of pseudogap. The highest Tc correlates with nuclear relaxation and Cu-O charge sharing rather than shift data. Relations to other probes are discussed.

title: Stealthy hyperuniform disorder: A new route to controlling electric states and magnetic phase transition in correlated systems
summary: We investigate the effects of stealthy hyperuniform bond distributions on the electronic and magnetic properties of the Hubbard model on the honeycomb lattice. Hyperuniform structures, distinct from random and quasiperiodic ones, have recently attracted considerable interest due to their anomalous suppression of density fluctuations. By diagonalizing the noninteracting Hamiltonian, we show that a linear density of states (DOS) robustly emerges, while the stealth property of the bond distribution changes the wave functions in the higher-energy region extended and significantly modifies the DOS near the band edge. To clarify the impact on magnetism, we apply the real-space Hartree approximation to the Hubbard model. We find that, the phase transition always occurs between semimetallic and antiferromagnetically ordered states and its critical interaction strength is sensitive to the stealth property. A comparison with the quasiperiodic honeycomb tiling further highlights the role of structural correlations. These results demonstrate that stealthy hyperuniform disorder provides a novel route to controlling electronic states and magnetic phase transitions in correlated systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates stealthy hyperuniform bond distributions on the Hubbard model on honeycomb lattice, showing they produce linear density of states and control magnetic phase transitions between semimetallic and antiferromagnetic states.
AI.motivation: Hyperuniform structures, which suppress density fluctuations differently from random or quasiperiodic systems, are emerging as important in condensed matter physics. However, their effects on correlated electronic systems like the Hubbard model remain unexplored. This study aims to understand how stealthy hyperuniform disorder can control electronic and magnetic properties.
AI.method:
1. Diagonalization of noninteracting Hamiltonian to study electronic properties
2. Real-space Hartree approximation for the interacting Hubbard model to study magnetic phase transitions
AI.result: A linear density of states (DOS) robustly emerges from the system. The stealth property modifies wave functions in higher-energy regions and significantly changes DOS near band edges. For magnetism, phase transitions occur between semimetallic and antiferromagnetically ordered states, with critical interaction strength being sensitive to the stealth property.
AI.conclusion: Stealthy hyperuniform disorder provides a novel route to controlling electronic states and magnetic phase transitions in correlated systems. This work highlights the potential of hyperuniform structures as a tuning mechanism in quantum materials.

title: Discovery of Graphene Sheets and C-Rich Micro-Oval structure in Stingless Bee Hive; Leading to an Emergent Material with Debut of Blue Emission
summary: Naturally produced stingless bee hive (NP-SBH) is an intricately produced material by the combination of waxes, resin and other biological materials that offers protection and structural stability to the bee colony. This study explores a detailed analysis of Indian stingless bee hive material using multi-characterization techniques approach to evaluate their morphological, ultrastructural, chemical composition and their crystallinity. FESEM reveal uniformly distributed micro-oval structures along with graphene sheets throughout the observed region. Furthermore, Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) provides the richness of carbon (C) in graphene as well as in the micro-oval structure. HRTEM gives an insight about the internal ultrastructure and arrangement of atoms in the sample which revealed the presence of multiple graphene sheets. The ring shape electron diffraction pattern and high resolution lattice fringes provide the arrangement of carbon atoms, with interlayer spacing (d) value 3.4 Å, well agreed with that of graphene. Furthermore, X-ray Diffraction (XRD) and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy support the presence of graphene. As a debut, we observe blue emission from PL spectroscopy with decay times 1.18 ns (42 %) and 5.41 ns (58 %).

AI.tldr: First study reporting discovery of natural graphene in stingless bee hive material through comprehensive materials characterization, revealing carbon-rich micro-oval structures and graphene sheets with interlayer spacing of 3.4 Å and blue photoluminescence emission.
AI.motivation: This study investigates the natural production of graphene-like materials in stingless bee hives. The motivation appears to be discovering natural sources of graphene and understanding the biogenic formation of carbon nanomaterials in biological systems.
AI.method: Multi-characterization approach including Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX), High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy.
AI.result: Researchers discovered graphene sheets in Indian stingless bee hive material with carbon-rich micro-oval structures. HRTEM revealed multiple graphene sheets with interlayer spacing of 3.4 Å, matching graphene's standard spacing. PL spectroscopy showed blue emission with decay times of 1.18 ns (42%) and 5.41 ns (58%).
AI.conclusion: This is the first report of naturally occurring graphene in stingless bee hive material, demonstrating that biological systems can produce graphene-like carbon structures. The findings open possibilities for understanding biogenic graphene formation and potential natural sources of carbon nanomaterials.

title: Unveiling the Superconducting Ground State of Heusler alloy Pd2ZrIn via muon spin relaxation and rotation measurement
summary: Full Heusler alloys XInPd2 (X= Zr, Hf and Ti) have recently attracted significant attention owing to their symmetry-driven electronic structure and also due to the interplay between disorder and emergent ground states. Within this family, Pd2ZrIn serves as a unique platform to study the effect of disorder on superconducting pairing. This alloy crystallizes in a cubic L21 structure with significant B2-type antisite disorder. Electrical resistivity and magnetic susceptibility studies confirm bulk type-II superconductivity with a transition temperature TC ~ 2.2 K. Zero-field μSR results reveal no evidence of spontaneous internal magnetic fields below TC, confirming the preservation of time-reversal symmetry. Transverse-field μSR spectra show the formation of a vortex lattice, consistent with type-II superconductivity, and the superfluid density is well described by a fully gapped, nodeless s-wave state with superconducting gap Δ (0) ~ 0.33 \pm 0.01 meV. Furthermore, the estimated ratio of transition temperature and Fermi temperature (TC/TF) indicates that this alloy lies within the conventional superconducting regime on the Uemura plot. These results establish Pd2ZrIn as a weakly coupled, dirty-limit, type-II superconductor; with a fully gapped, nodeless order parameter and preserved time-reversal symmetry.

AI.tldr: This study investigates superconductivity in the Heusler alloy Pd2ZrIn, which exhibits type-II superconductivity with Tc ~ 2.2 K. MuSR measurements confirm time-reversal symmetry preservation, a fully gapped nodeless s-wave order parameter, and dirty-limit superconductivity characteristics.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how disorder affects superconducting pairing in Heusler alloys, specifically using Pd2ZrIn as a model system to study the interplay between antisite disorder and emergent superconducting ground states.
AI.method: The methods employed include electrical resistivity measurements, magnetic susceptibility studies, and both zero-field and transverse-field muon spin rotation (μSR) spectroscopy to probe magnetic properties and vortex lattice formation.
AI.result: The results confirm bulk type-II superconductivity with Tc ~ 2.2 K. Zero-field μSR shows no spontaneous magnetic fields, preserving time-reversal symmetry. Transverse-field μSR reveals a vortex lattice consistent with type-II behavior. The superfluid density fits a fully gapped nodeless s-wave state with gap Δ(0) ~ 0.33 ± 0.01 meV. The Tc/TF ratio places it in the conventional regime of the Uemura plot.
AI.conclusion: Pd2ZrIn is established as a weakly coupled, dirty-limit, type-II superconductor with a fully gapped nodeless order parameter and preserved time-reversal symmetry.

title: Seniority Eigenstate Configuration Interaction
summary: Zero-seniority methods have shown great promise for the description of strongly-correlated electronic systems. Other seniority sectors have been much less explored, and in particular the maximal seniority sector and zero seniority have the same underlying algebraic structure. We introduce a seniority eigenstate configuration interaction in which the wave function is constrained to have good fixed local seniority for each paired orbital, by which we mean we partition orbitals into a pairing set with seniority zero, and a spin set with seniority one. We show how to build the effective Hamiltonian for this ansatz, and demonstrate that high-seniority wave functions have unexpectedly excellent accuracy for strongly-correlated fermionic systems, with accuracy competitive with or better than seniority zero for the Hubbard model and for the dissociation of the nitrogen molecule.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a seniority eigenstate configuration interaction method for strongly-correlated electronic systems, where wave functions maintain good fixed local seniority by partitioning orbitals into pairing set (seniority zero) and spin set (seniority one). The key finding is that high-seniority wave functions show unexpectedly excellent accuracy for the Hubbard model and nitrogen molecule dissociation, competitive with or better than seniority zero methods.
AI.motivation: Zero-seniority methods have shown promise for strongly-correlated systems, but other seniority sectors remain largely unexplored. The maximal seniority sector and zero seniority share the same underlying algebraic structure, motivating the exploration of high-seniority sectors.
AI.method: The authors introduce a seniority eigenstate configuration interaction approach where wave functions are constrained to have good fixed local seniority for each paired orbital. Orbitals are partitioned into a pairing set with seniority zero and a spin set with seniority one. The effective Hamiltonian is built for this ansatz.
AI.result: High-seniority wave functions demonstrate unexpectedly excellent accuracy for strongly-correlated fermionic systems. The accuracy is competitive with or better than seniority zero methods for both the Hubbard model and the dissociation of the nitrogen molecule.
AI.conclusion: The seniority eigenstate CI method provides a promising approach for describing strongly-correlated electronic systems. High-seniority sectors, previously less explored, can achieve excellent accuracy comparable to or better than zero-seniority methods.

title: Controlling Quantum Materials by Growth: Thermodynamics, Kinetics, and Defect Engineering in Transition Metal Dichalcogenides
summary:
Transition metal dichalcogenides exhibit a wide range of semiconducting, metallic, correlated, and topological electronic states that arise from strong coupling between lattice structure, dimensionality, and electronic degrees of freedom. In these materials, crystal growth is not merely a preparative step but a thermodynamic boundary condition that establishes chemical potentials, defect populations, polytype stability, and access to metastable phases. As a result, synthesis determines the structural and defect landscape from which collective electronic behavior emerges.
  In this Review, we develop a unified thermodynamic and kinetic framework that connects growth conditions to phase stability, defect energetics, and microstructure. We examine how chemical potential constraints define stability windows, how supersaturation and mass-transport regimes govern nucleation and morphology, and how nonequilibrium pathways enable kinetic trapping and polymorph selection. Bulk and thin-film synthesis approaches, including chemical vapor transport, flux growth, physical vapor transport, solvent-assisted crystallization, chemical vapor deposition, and molecular beam epitaxy, are placed within a common thermodynamic and kinetic map to clarify how distinct growth regimes produce characteristic disorder profiles and structural phases.
  By explicitly linking synthesis variables to charge-density-wave order, superconductivity, band topology, and correlation effects, this Review establishes crystal growth as a central parameter in determining the effective electronic Hamiltonian realized in experiment. This perspective provides a physically grounded framework for improving reproducibility and guiding deterministic control of emergent quantum phases in layered materials.

AI.tldr: This Review provides a unified thermodynamic and kinetic framework connecting TMD crystal synthesis methods to electronic properties. By explicitly linking growth conditions (chemical potentials, supersaturation, mass-transport) to phase stability, defect landscapes, and quantum phases (CDW, superconductivity, topology), the paper establishes crystal growth as a central parameter in determining effective electronic Hamiltonians.
AI.motivation: Crystal growth in TMDs is not merely preparative but establishes thermodynamic boundary conditions (chemical potentials, defect populations, polytype stability) that determine structural landscapes and collective electronic behavior. Existing approaches lacked connection between synthesis variables and electronic properties.
AI.method: Developed a unified thermodynamic and kinetic framework connecting growth conditions to phase stability, defect energetics, and microstructure. Examined chemical potential constraints, supersaturation regimes, and nonequilibrium pathways. Placed bulk/thin-film synthesis methods (CVT, flux growth, PVT, solvent-assisted, CVD, MBE) within a common thermodynamic and kinetic map.
AI.result: Explicitly linked synthesis variables to charge-density-wave order, superconductivity, band topology, and correlation effects. Characterized how distinct growth regimes produce characteristic disorder profiles and structural phases.
AI.conclusion: Crystal growth is established as a central parameter determining the effective electronic Hamiltonian. The framework enables improved reproducibility and guides deterministic control of emergent quantum phases in layered TMD materials.

title: Competing Constraints on Superconductivity in Thick FeSe films
summary: Superconducting films emerge from the complex interplay of multiple growth parameters, making their optimization challenging. In iron-based superconductors, compressive strain is known to enhance the transition temperature (Tc) of FeSe films, yet reported Tc values vary widely even on identical substrates, indicating factors beyond strain are critical. Here, we develop a high-throughput off-center pulsed laser deposition strategy that transforms plume inhomogeneity into combinatorial FeSe film libraries with continuous gradients in lattice parameter, composition, and disorder. We discover that the maximum Tc does not coincide with the plume center but can shift off-center, revealing a competition between favorable c-axis expansion, stoichiometry, and defect scattering. Systematic characterization of 80 thick films (>50 nm), combined with interpretable machine learning, shows that besides the strong correlate of c-axis lattice parameter to Tc, the stoichiometry and disorder scattering impose critical constraints on the achievable transition temperature, defining a narrow optimization window rather than a simple monotonic relationship. This framework yields Tconset=17.1 K in thick FeSe films and establishes a general framework combining combinatorial synthesis with machine learning to uncover constrained optimization landscapes in complex functional materials.

AI.tldr: The study develops a high-throughput combinatorial synthesis strategy for FeSe superconducting films and uses machine learning to discover that transition temperature is governed by a constrained optimization landscape where c-axis lattice parameter, stoichiometry, and defect scattering compete, achieving Tconset=17.1 K.
AI.motivation: Superconducting films emerge from complex interplay of multiple growth parameters, making optimization challenging. In FeSe films, compressive strain enhances Tc but reported values vary widely even on identical substrates, indicating factors beyond strain are critical.
AI.method: High-throughput off-center pulsed laser deposition strategy that transforms plume inhomogeneity into combinatorial FeSe film libraries with continuous gradients in lattice parameter, composition, and disorder. Systematic characterization of 80 thick films (>50 nm) combined with interpretable machine learning.
AI.result: Maximum Tc does not coincide with the plume center but shifts off-center, revealing a competition between favorable c-axis expansion, stoichiometry, and defect scattering. Besides the strong correlate of c-axis lattice parameter to Tc, stoichiometry and disorder scattering impose critical constraints on achievable Tc, defining a narrow optimization window rather than a simple monotonic relationship.
AI.conclusion: This framework yields Tconset=17.1 K in thick FeSe films and establishes a general framework combining combinatorial synthesis with machine learning to uncover constrained optimization landscapes in complex functional materials.

title: Re-examination of electronic structure of dilute Kondo transition-metal ions substituted into a Heavy Fermion compound
summary: Correlations between the localized and conductive spins/charges have been the central issue of various fascinating quantum phenomena found on itinerant electron systems. Here, the obvious multiplet structures are presented on the Mn 2$p$ to 3$d$ x-ray absorption for a heavy fermion $α$-(Yb,Lu)(Al$_{1-x}$Mn$_x$)B$_4$, indicating that the unoccupied electronic structure of the Mn site is described as the correlated high-spin 2+, even though magnetic measurements show the Mn sites to be nonmagnetic. This apparently paradoxical result demonstrates that a ligand field can effectively appear between localized Mn 3$d$ and surrounding B 2$p$ orbitals, which has been anticipated as a manifestation of a Kondo effect but not been clearly confirmed for most itinerant metals in spectroscopy. By contrast, the Mn 2$p$ photoemission indicates that the occupied Mn$^{2+}$ 3$d$ electrons still exhibit itinerant and nonlocally screened nature also owing to the Kondo-like correlation with the conductive B 2$p$, and heavier Yb 4$f$ and 5$d$ bands below the Fermi energy. The asymmetry on the particle-hole stimulates a reconsideration of the correlation and screening effects in the core-level spectroscopies.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Mn 2p-3d x-ray absorption and photoemission in heavy fermion α-(Yb,Lu)(Al₁₋ₓMnₓ)B₄, revealing paradoxical coexistence of correlated high-spin 2+ unoccupied states with nonmagnetic behavior in measurements, demonstrating ligand field formation as a manifestation of Kondo effect between localized Mn 3d and conductive B 2p orbitals.
AI.motivation: To investigate the correlations between localized Mn 3d spins/charges and conductive electrons in itinerant heavy fermion systems, specifically to confirm the anticipated manifestation of Kondo effect through ligand field formation between Mn 3d and B 2p orbitals.
AI.method: Mn 2p to 3d x-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and Mn 2p photoemission measurements were employed to investigate the electronic structure of Mn sites in heavy fermion α-(Yb,Lu)(Al₁₋ₓMnₓ)B₄.
AI.result: Multiplet structures in Mn 2p-3d XAS reveal that the unoccupied electronic structure at Mn sites exhibits correlated high-spin 2+ configuration, while magnetic measurements indicate nonmagnetic behavior at Mn sites. This paradox demonstrates effective ligand field formation between Mn 3d and B 2p orbitals. Photoemission shows occupied Mn²⁺ 3d electrons maintain itinerant and nonlocally screened nature due to Kondo-like correlations.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms that ligand fields can emerge between localized Mn 3d and B 2p orbitals as a manifestation of the Kondo effect, which was previously anticipated but not clearly confirmed in itinerant metals. The occupied Mn²⁺ 3d electrons exhibit itinerant and nonlocally screened nature due to Kondo-like correlations with conductive B 2p and heavier Yb 4f and 5d bands. This challenges conventional understanding of correlation and screening effects in core-level spectroscopies.

title: Electronic-Entropy-Driven Crossover to Close-Packed Phases in Transition Metals under Strong Electronic Excitation
summary: Solid-solid phase transitions in metals are traditionally governed by changes in density or external pressure. Here, we show that electronic entropy alone can control structural stability and drive phase transitions at fixed density across transition metals. Using finite-temperature density functional theory, we construct pressure-temperature phase diagrams for 15 metals spanning hcp, fcc, and bcc-ground-state structures. Despite their diverse ground-state behavior, all systems exhibit a common high-temperature response: structural stability collapses toward a reduced manifold dominated by close-packed phases, with fcc emerging as the predominant structure, hcp persisting as a secondary phase, and bcc stability strongly suppressed. This identifies a robust entropy-driven crossover that progressively erases ground-state structural specificity under strong electronic excitation. To elucidate the microscopic origin of this behavior, we perform a detailed analysis of manganese, where spin-dependent calculations with a Hubbard U correction capture the interplay between magnetism, electronic localization, and lattice stability. At low temperatures, phase competition is governed by magnetic order and on-site Coulomb interactions, whereas increasing electronic temperature leads to demagnetization, phonon hardening, and the emergence of an entropy-dominated regime consistent with the universal trends. We show that electronic entropy generates a substantial hot-electron thermal pressure that modifies interatomic forces and drives structural rearrangements at fixed density, producing lattice-dynamical effects analogous to hydrostatic compression. These results establish electronic entropy as a fundamental thermodynamic control parameter for structural transformations in metals and provide a unified framework for understanding phase stability under extreme electronic excitation.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that electronic entropy alone can drive structural phase transitions in transition metals at fixed density. Using finite-temperature DFT on 15 metals, researchers found a universal high-temperature behavior where close-packed phases (especially fcc) dominate over bcc structures. Electronic entropy generates thermal pressure that modifies interatomic forces, producing effects analogous to hydrostatic compression.
AI.motivation: Traditional understanding holds that solid-solid phase transitions in metals are governed by changes in density or external pressure. This study aims to demonstrate that electronic entropy alone can control structural stability and drive phase transitions at fixed density across transition metals.
AI.method: Finite-temperature density functional theory was used to construct pressure-temperature phase diagrams for 15 metals spanning hcp, fcc, and bcc ground-state structures. For manganese, spin-dependent calculations with a Hubbard U correction were performed to capture the interplay between magnetism, electronic localization, and lattice stability.
AI.result: All 15 transition metals exhibit a common high-temperature response: structural stability collapses toward a reduced manifold dominated by close-packed phases. fcc emerges as the predominant structure, hcp persists as a secondary phase, and bcc stability is strongly suppressed. For manganese specifically, phase competition at low temperatures is governed by magnetic order and on-site Coulomb interactions, while increasing electronic temperature leads to demagnetization, phonon hardening, and an entropy-dominated regime consistent with universal trends.
AI.conclusion: Electronic entropy is established as a fundamental thermodynamic control parameter for structural transformations in metals. The study provides a unified framework for understanding phase stability under extreme electronic excitation, showing that electronic entropy generates hot-electron thermal pressure that modifies interatomic forces and drives structural rearrangements at fixed density, producing lattice-dynamical effects analogous to hydrostatic compression.

title: Spatially-resolved voltage-reversal due to Bernoulli potentials in dissipative Bi$_2$Sr$_2$CaCu$_2$O$_{8+x}$
summary: We measure magneto-transport and critical currents in Bi$_2$Sr$_2$CaCu$_2$O$_{8+x}$ Hall bar devices. Above critical current in an applied magnetic field, we observe longitudinal differential voltage along one edge comparable in magnitude but opposite in sign to the other edge. This phenomenon is unaffected by reversal of the applied field, and seems unique to devices with invasive voltage contacts. We attribute the source of this behavior to particle-hole symmetry breaking in moving vortices and the formation of opposite Bernoulli potentials due to opposing vortex velocities at the edges where the invasive contacts create hotspots for rapid vortex nucleation and flux flow. These results are fundamental to the composition and flow of dissipative currents in layered superconductors.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates magneto-transport properties in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+x superconducting Hall bar devices, discovering an edge-dependent voltage asymmetry that reverses sign between opposite edges when current exceeds the critical current in a magnetic field. The authors attribute this to particle-hole symmetry breaking in moving vortices and the formation of opposing Bernoulli potentials at edge hotspots created by invasive voltage contacts.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand the fundamental mechanisms of dissipative currents in layered high-temperature superconductors, particularly focusing on vortex behavior and edge effects that have not been previously characterized in devices with invasive contacts.
AI.method: The researchers fabricated Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+x Hall bar devices and performed magneto-transport and critical current measurements. They specifically examined longitudinal differential voltages along device edges above the critical current under applied magnetic fields.
AI.result: They observed that above the critical current in an applied magnetic field, the longitudinal differential voltage along one edge has comparable magnitude but opposite sign to the voltage along the other edge. This phenomenon persists even when the applied field is reversed and appears unique to devices with invasive voltage contacts that create hotspots for rapid vortex nucleation and flux flow.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal that particle-hole symmetry breaking in moving vortices and the formation of opposite Bernoulli potentials due to opposing vortex velocities at edges are fundamental to understanding the composition and flow of dissipative currents in layered superconductors. This provides new insight into vortex dynamics and edge effects in high-Tc superconductors.

title: Triple-${\bf Q}$ collinear state with compensated ferrimagnetic nature on frustrated kagome lattice
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, accepted for publication in npj Spintronics
summary: Spin-selective band splitting without net magnetization and spin-orbit couplings serves for a next-generation spin-current generator, and its typical platforms are altermagnets and compensated ferrimagnets as well, where the existence of a crystal asymmetry or nonequivalent sites is essential. Here, we theoretically demonstrate that such a splitting can be realized in a triple-{\bf Q} 12-sublattice state emerging in a $J_3$-dominant kagome antiferromagnet, without the help of the crystal asymmetry. Reflecting the multi-sublattice nature, a local magnetization reveals a fully compensated ferrimagnetic pattern in units of a triangle plaquette, leading to $s$-wave-type spin splittings in magnon and electron bands. This enables an atiferromagnetic spin Seebeck effect at zero field in insulating systems and filling-controlled polarized states in metallic systems, highlighting the potential of frustrated magnets to realize novel spintronics functionalities.

AI.tldr: Theoretical demonstration of spin-selective band splitting in a triple-Q 12-sublattice state emerging in a J_3-dominant kagome antiferromagnet, achieving s-wave-type spin splittings in magnon and electron bands without crystal asymmetry or net magnetization.
AI.motivation: Conventional spin-current generators rely on altermagnets or compensated ferrimagnets requiring crystal asymmetry or nonequivalent sites. This work seeks to realize spin-selective band splitting in frustrated magnets without these requirements, exploring novel spintronics functionalities.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of a triple-Q 12-sublattice state in a J_3-dominant kagome antiferromagnet, examining local magnetization patterns and spin splittings in both magnon and electron bands.
AI.result: The multi-sublattice nature produces a fully compensated ferrimagnetic pattern in triangle plaquette units, enabling s-wave-type spin splittings in magnon and electron bands without net magnetization or spin-orbit couplings.
AI.conclusion: This work highlights the potential of frustrated magnets to realize novel spintronics functionalities, including antiferromagnetic spin Seebeck effect at zero field in insulating systems and filling-controlled polarized states in metallic systems.

title: Rippled graphene pores as fluidic memristive devices with synaptic and neuromorphic functionalities
summary: Nanofluidic memristive devices work with nanoscale pores and ions dissolved in water, which harness the ionic memory effect aiming to store and process information. These devices share the same charge carriers as biological systems and bring hope for better emulating the neural functions and developing ionic circuits for neuromorphic applications. Specially, theory and experiments suggest that nanoconfinement is essential for inducing a memory effect, which places limit on the pore size to nm-scale or smaller. Such devices are difficult to scale up with precision and operate with long-term stability. Here, we show that a micrometer size pore, generally expected to exhibit a linear ion transport, can display a pronounced memory effect, if its rim is wrapped by strongly curved and tightly stacked graphene. We attribute the observation to slow ion dynamics confined in the rippled graphene edges. The devices are easy to scale up and integrate into fluidic circuits. The memory effect is ion-selective and exhibits long endurance comparable to the lifetime of synaptic proteins, which enables reversible modification of the conductance states using programmable voltage spikes and various electrolytes over a long time, akin to biological synaptic plasticity. Thanks to this plasticity, our devices and their integrated circuits enable storing, transmitting and processing information with high reliability, fidelity and accuracy, as evidenced in the identification of both greyscale and color images, and in the real-time analysis of emulated neural signals. Our results highlight nanoscale morphology of the pore wall as an important parameter regulating ion transport and indicate that the stringent nanoconfinement for ionic memory can be lifted from restricting the pore size to designing its rim structure. The devices and their integrated circuits may find use in ionic neuromorphic applications.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that micrometer-sized pores with graphene-wrapped rims can exhibit ionic memory effects, bypassing the previous requirement for nanoscale pores. The devices show synaptic-like plasticity, long-term stability, and enable reliable information processing for neuromorphic applications.
AI.motivation: Nanofluidic memristive devices requiring nm-scale pores are difficult to scale up and lack long-term stability, limiting their practical applications for ionic neuromorphic computing.
AI.method: Wrapping micrometer-sized pore rims with strongly curved and tightly stacked graphene; studying ion transport dynamics, ion-selectivity, and memory effects; testing devices with programmable voltage spikes and various electrolytes; implementing integrated circuits for image identification and neural signal analysis.
AI.result: Micrometer pores with graphene-wrapped rims exhibit pronounced memory effects due to slow ion dynamics in rippled graphene edges. The devices show ion-selectivity, long endurance (comparable to synaptic protein lifetime), and enable reversible conductance modification. Integrated circuits successfully identified greyscale and color images and performed real-time analysis of emulated neural signals.
AI.conclusion: The stringent nanoconfinement requirement for ionic memory can be overcome by designing the pore rim structure (with curved, stacked graphene) rather than restricting pore size to nanoscale. This enables scalable, stable ionic neuromorphic devices and circuits for information storage, transmission, and processing.

title: Observation of field-odd and field-free superconducting diode effects in $\mathrm{Mo}_2\mathrm{C}$ nanoflakes
summary: The superconducting diode effect (SDE) enables nonreciprocal supercurrent flow, holding immense potential for ultra-low-power quantum electronics. Intrinsic SDE typically requires materials with inherent symmetry breakings. Here, we report the discovery of SDE in chemical vapor deposition-grown molybdenum carbide ($\mathrm{Mo}_2\mathrm{C}$) nanoflakes, a material traditionally considered centrosymmetric. Strikingly, this system uniquely hosts both field-odd and field-free SDEs. Transport measurements reveal a field-odd SDE with tunable efficiency exceeding 40% at 4 K under a perpendicular in-plane magnetic field. In a separate sample, a robust field-free SDE persists under zero-field and field-coolings. Out-of-plane field sweeps confirm the intrinsic nature of these phenomena. We propose that domain-boundary supercurrents or charge density wave-like orders drive this unexpected combination of symmetry breakings. Our findings establish air-stable $\mathrm{Mo}_2\mathrm{C}$ as an ideal platform for nonreciprocal superconducting electronics operating at liquid-helium temperatures, expanding the search for SDE into nominally centrosymmetric superconductors.
