The field-odd SDE achieved >40% efficiency at 4K, while field-free SDE was observed in separate samples. Domain-boundary supercurrents or CDW-like orders are proposed as mechanisms, establishing Mo2C as a promising platform for nonreciprocal superconducting electronics.
AI.motivation: The superconducting diode effect (SDE) enables nonreciprocal supercurrent flow with potential for ultra-low-power quantum electronics. Intrinsic SDE typically requires materials with inherent symmetry breakings. This study aimed to investigate SDE in Mo2C, a material traditionally considered centrosymmetric, to explore whether nominally centrosymmetric superconductors can exhibit SDE.
AI.method: Transport measurements were conducted on chemical vapor deposition-grown Mo2C nanoflakes. The study examined both field-odd SDE under perpendicular in-plane magnetic field and field-free SDE under zero-field and field-cooling conditions. Out-of-plane field sweeps were performed to confirm the intrinsic nature of the phenomena.
AI.result: The study discovered both field-odd and field-free SDEs in Mo2C nanoflakes. The field-odd SDE showed tunable efficiency exceeding 40% at 4 K under perpendicular in-plane magnetic field. A separate sample exhibited robust field-free SDE persisting under zero-field and field-coolings. Out-of-plane field sweeps confirmed the intrinsic nature of these phenomena.

The proposed mechanisms (domain-boundary supercurrents or CDW-like orders) explain the unexpected combination of symmetry breakings.

title: Spin-orbit-induced quantum chiral phases
summary: The scalar spin chirality (SSC), whose nonzero value $\langle {\bf S}_i \cdot ({\bf S}_j \times {\bf S}_k) \rangle \neq 0$ implies the breaking of time-reversal and certain point-group symmetries in the ground state, is a key quantity characterizing chiral magnetism in both classical and quantum settings. The classical SSC is manifested, for instance, in skyrmion crystal phase, while the quantum SSC is still highly sought after in various frustrated spin-1/2 models. An interesting possibility that has not been explored so far is the case in which SSC is symmetry-wise allowed, yet remains zero classically due to the collinear or coplanar arrangement of spins, but is generated by virtue of quantum fluctuation. We demonstrate the existence of precisely such a phase in a spin-1/2 triangular-lattice model with XXZ interaction, spin-orbit-induced exchange interactions, and an external magnetic field. Using iDMRG, we thoroughly map out the phase diagram of the model and identify several phases with coexisting magnetic order and SSC. The nonzero SSC arises despite the classical magnetic order being collinear or coplanar. We provide detailed magnon analysis to ascribe its origin to quantum fluctuations around classical magnetic order. The estimate of SSC from magnon analysis agrees with iDMRG results quantitatively. We map out the magnon spectrum and its Berry curvature, culminating in the prediction of finite thermal Hall conductivity in these phases with SSC.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers a new phase in a spin-1/2 triangular-lattice model where scalar spin chirality (SSC) is generated by quantum fluctuations despite the classical magnetic order being collinear or coplanar. Using iDMRG and magnon analysis, the authors map out the phase diagram, find coexisting magnetic order and SSC, and predict finite thermal Hall conductivity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the case where scalar spin chirality (SSC) is symmetry-wise allowed but remains zero classically due to collinear or coplanar spin arrangements, yet can be generated through quantum fluctuations—a phenomenon that had not been explored before.
AI.method: The authors employed infinite Density Matrix Renormalization Group (iDMRG) to map out the phase diagram of a spin-1/2 triangular-lattice model with XXZ interaction, spin-orbit-induced exchange interactions, and an external magnetic field. They supplemented this with detailed magnon analysis around classical magnetic order to understand the origin of SSC.
AI.result: The authors identified several phases with coexisting magnetic order and SSC in the model. The nonzero SSC arises despite the classical magnetic order being collinear or coplanar. The magnon analysis estimate of SSC agrees quantitatively with iDMRG results. The study also predicts finite thermal Hall conductivity in these SSC phases based on magnon spectrum and Berry curvature analysis.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a novel mechanism for generating scalar spin chirality through quantum fluctuations in a collinear or coplanar classical ordered state. Magnon analysis successfully explains the origin of SSC and predicts finite thermal Hall conductivity in these phases, with quantitative agreement between theoretical estimates and iDMRG calculations.

title: Electrically steered conduction topologies and period-doubling phase dynamics in VO2
summary: The insulator-to-metal transition (IMT) in strongly correlated materials, such as vanadium dioxide (VO2), offers a transformative platform for next-generation adaptive electronics and neuromorphic computing. However, harnessing this non-equilibrium phase transition for deterministic device operation is fundamentally hindered by the inability to disentangle electric-field effects from Joule heating, owing to a lack of operando techniques capable of resolving phase dynamics at nanoscale spatial and sub-nanosecond temporal scales. Here, using a newly developed electrical-pulse-pump ultrafast transmission electron microscope (E-UTEM), we directly visualize the multi-scale electro-thermo-mechanical dynamics of the IMT in suspended VO2 devices. Our results reveal that electric-field-induced Poole-Frenkel (PF) emission, localized by patterned oxygen vacancies, plays a decisive role in redistributing the internal electric field to trigger a deterministic Mott transition. The extreme non-linearity of this PF effect enables the formation of dynamically reconfigurable connectivity topologies that bypass conventional thermal limits. Furthermore, we observe that the coupling of thermal and elastic energies governs a discrete domain evolution, characterized by step-wise and period-doubling configurational resets, which is a hallmark of non-equilibrium phase dynamics in constrained geometries. By integrating experimental imaging with phase-field simulations, we establish a comprehensive framework for the electrically-driven IMT and predict sub-100-ps switching kinetics. These findings provide a fundamental basis for the rational design of ultrafast, low-energy functional devices through nanoscale defect and strain engineering in correlated systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces E-UTEM technique to directly visualize the insulator-to-metal transition in VO2 at nanoscale and sub-nanosecond scales, revealing that Poole-Frenkel emission from patterned oxygen vacancies triggers deterministic Mott transitions and enables dynamically reconfigurable connectivity. Thermal-elastic coupling drives discrete domain evolution with period-doubling behavior, establishing a framework for sub-100-ps switching and ultrafast low-energy device design.
AI.motivation: The insulator-to-metal transition (IMT) in strongly correlated materials like VO2 offers transformative potential for adaptive electronics and neuromorphic computing, but harnessing this non-equilibrium phase transition is hindered by inability to disentangle electric-field effects from Joule heating due to lack of operando techniques capable of resolving phase dynamics at nanoscale spatial and sub-nanosecond temporal scales.
AI.method: Developed and applied electrical-pulse-pump ultrafast transmission electron microscope (E-UTEM) to directly visualize multi-scale electro-thermo-mechanical dynamics of IMT in suspended VO2 devices. Combined experimental imaging with phase-field simulations.
AI.result: Revealed that electric-field-induced Poole-Frenkel (PF) emission, localized by patterned oxygen vacancies, plays a decisive role in redistributing internal electric field to trigger deterministic Mott transition. The extreme nonlinearity of PF effect enables formation of dynamically reconfigurable connectivity topologies bypassing conventional thermal limits. Coupling of thermal and elastic energies governs discrete domain evolution characterized by step-wise and period-doubling configurational resets - a hallmark of non-equilibrium phase dynamics in constrained geometries.
AI.conclusion: Established a comprehensive framework for electrically-driven IMT by integrating experimental imaging with phase-field simulations, predicting sub-100-ps switching kinetics. This provides a fundamental basis for rational design of ultrafast, low-energy functional devices through nanoscale defect and strain engineering in correlated systems.

title: Superconducting properties of the three-dimensional Hofstadter-Hubbard model below the critical flux for Weyl points
summary: The three-dimensional Hofstadter model exhibits a critical rational flux at which Weyl points emerge in the single-particle spectrum. We study the superconducting regime of the model in the presence of a Hubbard attractive interaction by tuning the magnetic flux across its critical value. We determine the phase diagram in the plane of the coprime pairs parametrizing the magnetic flux. We show that the system exhibits two distinct regimes separated by a critical flux $Φ_c$: for $Φ>Φ_c$, a semimetal-to-superconductor quantum phase transition occurs at a finite interaction strength ($U_c\neq0$), while for $Φ<Φ_c$ superconductivity arises for arbitrarily weak attraction, with a BCS-like exponential scaling of the gap due to the finiteness of the density of states. Close to the transition, we study the scaling behavior and identify the critical exponents. Our results highlight the interplay between magnetic band topology and attractive pairing in three-dimensional Hofstadter systems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the superconducting phase of the three-dimensional Hofstadter model with attractive Hubbard interaction near the critical flux where Weyl points emerge. They find two distinct regimes: for Φ > Φc, a semimetal-to-superconductor transition occurs at finite interaction Uc ≠ 0; for Φ < Φc, superconductivity emerges for arbitrarily weak attraction with BCS-like exponential gap scaling. Critical exponents are identified near the transition.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by exploring the interplay between magnetic band topology (Weyl points) and attractive pairing interactions in three-dimensional Hofstadter systems, particularly at the critical rational flux where Weyl points emerge in the single-particle spectrum.
AI.method: The researchers tune the magnetic flux across its critical value and determine the phase diagram in the plane of coprime pairs parametrizing the magnetic flux. They analyze scaling behavior near the quantum phase transition and identify critical exponents.
AI.result: The system exhibits two distinct regimes separated by a critical flux Φc: (1) For Φ > Φc, a semimetal-to-superconductor quantum phase transition occurs at finite interaction strength Uc ≠ 0; (2) For Φ < Φc, superconductivity arises for arbitrarily weak attraction with BCS-like exponential scaling of the gap due to finite density of states. Critical exponents are identified near the transition.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the interplay between magnetic band topology and attractive pairing in three-dimensional Hofstadter systems, showing that the presence of Weyl points fundamentally changes the superconducting behavior - enabling superconductivity at arbitrarily weak interactions when the flux exceeds the critical value.

title: Four-layer charge density waves and chirality in CsV$_3$Sb$_5$
summary: The kagome superconductor CsV$_3$Sb$_5$ is the only one in the AV$_3$Sb$_5$ family (A=K,Rb,Cs) that shows a $2\times2\times4$ charge-density-wave (CDW) ground state competing with the more common $2\times2\times2$. In addition, it is also the only one that shows second-harmonic transport and thus broken inversion symmetry, suggesting these two features are connected. In this work, we test whether the $2\times2\times4$ CDW can break inversion symmetry by analyzing its stacking energetics with a real-space Ginzburg-Landau free energy. In the limit where each layer is forced to adopt a fixed, threefold symmetric tri-hexagonal configuration, we find an analytical phase diagram mostly occupied by inversion preserving AB and ABCD stacked solutions. Relaxing this rigid layer constraint in a physically meaningful way consistent with \emph{ab initio}--calculated parameters, we find several new phases including an AABC solution and a distorted ABCD solution, which break inversion and all mirrors and are thus chiral. However, these phases occupy only small fractions of phase space, suggesting other mechanisms for inversion symmetry breaking may be at play in CsV$_3$Sb$_5$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether the 2×2×4 charge-density-wave (CDW) in the kagome superconductor CsV3Sb5 can break inversion symmetry, finding that while chiral phases exist that break inversion and all mirrors, these occupy only small fractions of phase space.
AI.motivation: CsV3Sb5 is unique in the AV3Sb5 family for showing both a 2×2×4 CDW ground state and second-harmonic transport (broken inversion symmetry), suggesting these features may be connected.
AI.method: Real-space Ginzburg-Landau free energy analysis was used to analyze stacking energetics, first with a rigid threefold symmetric tri-hexagonal layer constraint, then relaxing this constraint using physically meaningful parameters from ab initio calculations.
AI.result: With rigid layer constraints, inversion-preserving AB and ABCD stacked solutions dominate. With relaxed constraints, new phases including AABC and distorted ABCD solutions emerge that break inversion and all mirrors (chiral), but these occupy only small fractions of phase space.
AI.conclusion: The 2×2×4 CDW can theoretically break inversion symmetry in certain configurations, but the small phase space coverage suggests other mechanisms may be primarily responsible for inversion symmetry breaking in CsV3Sb5.

title: True random number generation through stochastic magnonic bistability
summary: True random number generators (TRNGs) underpin modern cryptography, yet existing implementations face fundamental trade-offs between speed, scalability, and entropy quality. Here, we demonstrate that stochastic switching in the bistable regime of spin-wave dynamics provides a physical entropy source for high-quality random number generation. Our magnonic random number generator (mRNG), based on a lithography-patterned microstrip on yttrium iron garnet (YIG), exploits thermal fluctuations near the nonlinear bistable regime to generate random bitstreams that pass all 15 NIST SP 800-22 statistical tests at rates with 20 Mb/s. We implement a random-bit multiplier using synchronized mRNG units and demonstrate scalability to 200-nm-wide nanoscale waveguides, establishing spin-wave bistability as a viable physical entropy source for integrated random number generation.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates a magnonic random number generator (mRNG) using stochastic switching in the bistable regime of spin-wave dynamics in YIG. It achieves 20 Mb/s random bit generation passing all NIST SP 800-22 tests and can be scaled to nanoscale waveguides.
AI.motivation: Existing true random number generators face fundamental trade-offs between speed, scalability, and entropy quality. There is a need for a physical entropy source that can deliver high-quality random numbers at high rates while remaining suitable for integrated circuit implementation.
AI.method: The researchers developed a magnonic random number generator (mRNG) using a lithography-patterned microstrip on yttrium iron garnet (YIG). They exploited thermal fluctuations near the nonlinear bistable regime of spin-wave dynamics as the physical entropy source. A random-bit multiplier was implemented using synchronized mRNG units to demonstrate scalability.
AI.result: The mRNG generates random bitstreams that pass all 15 NIST SP 800-22 statistical tests at rates of 20 Mb/s. The device was successfully scaled to 200-nm-wide nanoscale waveguides, demonstrating scalability.
AI.conclusion: Spin-wave bistability represents a promising physical entropy source for integrated random number generation, with potential for on-chip TRNG implementations. The successful scaling to 200-nm waveguides demonstrates feasibility for miniaturized cryptographic applications.

title: Multimodal Transformer for Sample-Aware Prediction of Metal-Organic Framework Properties
summary: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a major target of machine-learning-based property prediction, yet most models assume that a single framework representation maps to a single property value. This assumption becomes problematic for experimental MOFs, where samples reported as the same framework can exhibit different properties because of differences in crystallinity, phase purity, defects, and other sample-dependent factors. Here we introduce Experimental X-ray Diffraction Integrated Transformer (EXIT), a multimodal transformer for sample-aware prediction of MOF properties that combines MOFid with X-ray diffraction (XRD). In EXIT, MOFid encodes MOF identity, whereas XRD provides complementary information about the experimentally realized sample state. EXIT is pre-trained on one million hypothetical MOFs with simulated XRD to learn transferable representations, leading to improved downstream performance relative to existing approaches. EXIT is fine-tuned on literature-derived experimental datasets for surface area and pore volume prediction. Incorporating experimental XRD improves predictive performance relative to models without experimental XRD, and attention analysis and sample-level case studies further show that EXIT assigns different predictions to samples sharing the same MOF identity when their XRD patterns differ. These results establish a practical step from framework-aware to sample-aware MOF property prediction and highlight the value of incorporating experimental characterization into porous materials informatics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces EXIT, a multimodal transformer that combines MOFid (framework identity) with experimental X-ray diffraction (XRD) for sample-aware property prediction of metal-organic frameworks. Unlike traditional ML models that assume one framework equals one property, EXIT accounts for experimental variations in real MOF samples.
AI.motivation: Traditional machine learning models for MOF property prediction assume a single framework representation maps to a single property value. However, experimental MOFs with the same framework identity can exhibit different properties due to variations in crystallinity, phase purity, defects, and other sample-dependent factors. This limitation creates a gap between computational predictions and experimental realities.
AI.method: The authors developed EXIT, a multimodal transformer that combines two data sources: (1) MOFid to encode MOF identity, and (2) experimental XRD patterns to capture information about the realized sample state. The model was pre-trained on one million hypothetical MOFs with simulated XRD to learn transferable representations, then fine-tuned on literature-derived experimental datasets for surface area and pore volume prediction.
AI.result: Incorporating experimental XRD improved predictive performance compared to models without experimental XRD. Attention analysis and case studies demonstrated that EXIT assigns different predictions to samples sharing the same MOF identity when their XRD patterns differ, validating the model's ability to capture sample-specific information.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a practical advancement from framework-aware to sample-aware MOF property prediction, highlighting the value of incorporating experimental characterization data into porous materials informatics. The approach addresses a fundamental limitation in MOF machine learning by accounting for real-world sample variations.

title: Field-induced antiferromagnetic transition in CeIrIn$_5$
comment: Accepted for publication as a Letter in Phys. Rev B
summary: We report low-temperature $^{115}$In nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) study of the prototypical heavy-fermion compound CeIrIn$_5$ in high magnetic fields applied close to the crystallographic $c$ axis. For this orientation, a field-induced transition was previously reported to take place at about 28 T. Although we do not observe any change of the NMR spectrum above the transition, the intensity of the NMR lines drastically decreases as a consequence of a considerable shortening of the $T_2$ relaxation time. In addition, $1/T_1$ shows a pronounced maximum at the transition. Taking into account previous high-field de Haas-van Alphen results in conjunction with band-structure calculations, our NMR results are most naturally explained by the field-induced transition into an antiferromagnetic state with the propagation vector $\mathbf{Q} = (1/2, 1/2, 0)$ and magnetic moments aligned antiferromagnetically along the $c$ axis. This makes CeIrIn$_5$ a unique case where the application of the magnetic field induces an ordered state with moments antiferromagnetically aligned along the field direction.

AI.tldr: This paper reports low-temperature 115In NMR studies of CeIrIn5 heavy-fermion compound in high magnetic fields, revealing a field-induced transition at ~28 T characterized by decreased NMR intensity due to shortened T2 relaxation and a maximum in 1/T1, interpreted as transition to an antiferromagnetic state with Q=(1/2,1/2,0) and moments aligned along c-axis.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to investigate a field-induced transition at ~28 T in CeIrIn5 when magnetic field is applied near the c-axis, using NMR spectroscopy to understand the nature of this transition which was previously detected by dHvA measurements but whose origin was unclear.
AI.method: The researchers performed low-temperature 115In nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements on CeIrIn5 in high magnetic fields applied close to the c-axis, measuring NMR spectral lines, intensity, T2 relaxation time, and 1/T1 spin-lattice relaxation rate.
AI.result: No change in NMR spectrum was observed above the transition, but NMR intensity drastically decreased due to shortened T2 relaxation time, and 1/T1 showed a pronounced maximum at the transition field. Combined with previous dHvA data and band-structure calculations, the transition is interpreted as field-induced antiferromagnetic ordering with propagation vector Q=(1/2,1/2,0) and antiferromagnetically aligned moments along the c-axis.
AI.conclusion: CeIrIn5 represents a unique case where applying a magnetic field induces an ordered antiferromagnetic state with moments aligned antiferromagnetically along the field direction. This is an unusual and interesting phenomenon where the field itself stabilizes antiferromagnetic order with moments parallel to the field.

title: Operando Characterization of Volume Changes in Lithium-Ion Battery Electrodes during Cycling using Isotope Multilayers
summary: This study reports on advancements in operando characterization of volume changes in lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrode materials during electrochemical cycling. Volume changes are crucial for LIB operation because they are related to the amount of stored energy as well as LIB integrity, performance, and safety. The study introduces a method based on isotope multilayers as active material to track the intrinsic modification of electrode volume in real time under operating conditions with operando neutron reflectometry. A natGe-73Ge multilayer film is used as a model system to measure the volume change of amorphous germanium electrodes during charging and discharging. Isotope modulation produces a Bragg peak in the neutron reflectivity pattern, sensitive only to the modification of volume within the active material of the electrode. Battery side reactions, such as the growth and reduction of the solid-electrolyte interphase is excluded. Using this method, the volume modification as a function of Li content x in LixGe can easily be derived from the scattering vector position of the Bragg peak without fitting numerous complex reflectivity patterns. The experiments show a reversible volume change of amorphous germanium of up to 250 percent for x = 3, which appears to be largely independent of current density, cycle number, and the thickness of the individual Ge layers. Also, there are tentative indications that the crystallization and re-amorphization of LixGe are not influencing the volume change.

title: Hydrodynamics of the viscous electron fluid in cadmium
summary: Thanks to electron-electron ($e$-$e$) collisions conserving momentum, metallic electron fluids are viscous. Yet, this viscosity is rarely detectable in bulk transport. Here, we report on the canonical realization of the Gurzhi effect in an elemental three-dimensional metal: cadmium. Using focused ion beam microstructuring to tune the effective thickness, we detected a low-temperature size-dependent resistivity upturn in a finite window sandwiched between ballistic and diffusive regimes. Within this window, the electrical conductivity displays a simultaneous quadratic dependence on both sample size and temperature -- fingerprint of a hydrodynamic flow. This leads us to quantify the amplitude and the temperature dependence of kinematic and dynamic viscosity of the electron fluid. In cadmium, in contrast with graphene and $^3$He, the rate of momentum-conserving $e$-$e$ collisions is not set by the main Fermi energy, but by Lilliputian energy scales and inter-valley bottlenecks.

title: Lattice thermal transport from phonon spectra beyond perturbation theory
summary: We develop a molecular dynamics framework to compute the mode-resolved phonon spectral density from classical correlations of an annihilation-like phonon variable. For harmonic oscillators, classical molecular dynamics exactly reproduces the corresponding quantum Kubo-transformed correlator, providing the basis for extension to anharmonic systems. Using PbTe as a benchmark and Cs$_3$Bi$_2$I$_6$Cl$_3$ as a strongly anharmonic test case, we show that the method captures both quasiparticle and non-Lorentzian spectra beyond perturbative quasiparticle theory, while yielding thermal conductivity in good agreement with experiment. This framework provides a direct route from classical molecular dynamics to quantum-mechanical Wigner heat transport in solids.

AI.tldr: A molecular dynamics framework is developed to compute mode-resolved phonon spectral density from classical correlations, enabling prediction of thermal conductivity in solids with strong anharmonicity.
AI.motivation: Current methods for computing phonon transport in solids struggle with strongly anharmonic systems. The goal is to develop a method that can capture non-Lorentzian spectra and quasiparticle behavior beyond perturbative quasiparticle theory, while providing a direct route from classical molecular dynamics to quantum-mechanical Wigner heat transport.
AI.method: Classical molecular dynamics is used to compute correlations of an annihilation-like phonon variable. For harmonic oscillators, this exactly reproduces the quantum Kubo-transformed correlator. The method is benchmarked with PbTe (a well-studied system) and tested with Cs₃Bi₂I₆Cl₃ (a strongly anharmonic material).
AI.result: The method successfully captures both quasiparticle and non-Lorentzian phonon spectra beyond perturbative quasiparticle theory. Thermal conductivity calculations show good agreement with experimental measurements for both benchmark and test cases.
AI.conclusion: This molecular dynamics framework provides a direct and computationally tractable route from classical simulations to quantum-mechanical Wigner heat transport in solids, enabling study of strongly anharmonic materials where perturbative methods fail.

title: Termination-Controlled Fractionalization and Hybridization at Topological Interfaces in Organic Spin Chains
summary: A single organic spin platform hosts both dimerized $S=\tfrac{1}{2}$ and effective Haldane $S=1$ sectors, linked by bond-texture inversion. At the junction, the fractional mode is controlled by termination parity: quenched by local fusion at one termination and released as an uncompensated spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$-like degree of freedom at the parity-shifted one. Two such internal boundary modes of a finite embedded Haldane domain hybridize with an exponentially decaying splitting, establishing termination parity as a design principle for engineering and coupling fractional boundary modes.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that termination parity (whether a spin chain terminates on an even or odd site) controls fractional boundary modes at junctions between dimerized S=1/2 and Haldane S=1 phases. One termination quenches the fractional mode via local fusion, while the other preserves it as an uncompensated spin-1/2 degree of freedom. Multiple boundary modes can hybridize with exponentially decaying splitting, establishing termination parity as a design principle for engineering topological boundary states in organic magnetic materials.
AI.motivation: This paper addresses the engineering and control of fractional boundary modes in spin systems, specifically exploring the interplay between dimerized S=1/2 and effective Haldane S=1 phases in an organic spin platform. The key insight is that termination parity (whether the chain ends on an even or odd site) can serve as a design principle to control these fractional modes - either quenching them or preserving them as uncompensated spin-1/2 degrees of freedom.
AI.method: The study appears to use theoretical/simulation methods involving dimerization, bond-texture inversion (modifying the bonding pattern), and analysis of boundary states at junctions between different spin phases. The key controllable parameter is "termination parity" - the way the chain ends determine the fate of the fractional mode.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that: (1) the fractional boundary mode is controlled by termination parity - one termination via local fusion quenches it, while parity-shifted termination preserves it as spin-1/2-like; (2) two internal boundary modes of a finite embedded Haldane domain hybridize with exponentially decaying splitting; (3) termination parity can serve as a design principle for engineering and coupling fractional boundary modes.
AI.conclusion: The authors establish termination parity as a viable design principle for engineering fractional boundary modes in spin systems. This provides a pathway to deliberately control and couple these modes, with implications for topological quantum computation and understanding quantum phase transitions in magnetic materials.

title: Electronic structure and oxidation states in high-pressure synthesized isostructural CeCN$_5$ and TbCN$_5$
summary: Understanding the behavior of 4$f$ electrons in materials containing rare earth elements is one of the fundamental questions within condensed matter physics. In this work the electronic properties of isostructural CeCN$_5$ and TbCN$_5$, both recently synthesized at extreme pressure, are investigated using Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations. We include the on-site Coulomb repulsion between localized 4$f$ states within the static DFT+U framework; the DFT+U results are cross-checked with DFT+dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) calculations within the quasi-atomic (Hubbard-I) approximation. Despite CeCN$_5$ and TbCN$_5$ being isostructural compounds Ce and Tb show different oxidation states, 4+ and 3+ respectively. This leads to distinctly different electronic properties: the former compound is an insulator, while the latter is a metal. An extra electron which is donated by Ce to the polymeric C-N network is distributed across the network. This leads to a modification of the bond length in CeCN$_5$ compared to TbCN$_5$. Still, the polymeric C-N networks can accommodate the different oxidation states in isostructural lanthanide-carbon-nitrogen (LnCN) compounds. Our results underline that LnCN compounds under high pressure offer a unique platform for probing the interplay between 4$f$-electron behavior and structural complexity.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the electronic properties of isostructural CeCN5 and TbCN5 using DFT+U and DFT+DMFT methods. Despite similar crystal structures, Ce and Tb exhibit different oxidation states (4+ vs 3+), resulting in CeCN5 being an insulator and TbCN5 being a metal. The polymeric C-N network can accommodate these different oxidation states.
AI.motivation: Understanding the behavior of 4f electrons in materials containing rare earth elements is one of the fundamental questions in condensed matter physics
AI.method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations with DFT+U framework, cross-checked with DFT+dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) within the quasi-atomic (Hubbard-I) approximation
AI.result: CeCN5 is an insulator while TbCN5 is a metal, despite being isostructural. Ce shows 4+ oxidation state while Tb shows 3+ oxidation state. An extra electron donated by Ce to the C-N network modifies bond lengths in CeCN5 compared to TbCN5.
AI.conclusion: LnCN compounds under high pressure provide a unique platform for studying the interplay between 4f-electron behavior and structural complexity. The polymeric C-N network can accommodate different oxidation states in isostructural lanthanide-carbon-nitrogen compounds.

title: Atomic-scale origin of charge density wave-driven metal-semiconductor transition in an incommensurately modulated metal-organic framework
summary: The intrinsic incommensurate charge density wave in metal-organic frameworks has remained elusive due to the lack of direct evidence linking atomic-scale structural modulation to macroscopic electronic properties. Using high-quality Pr3HHTP2 (HHTP = 2,3,6,7,10,11-hexahydroxytriphenylene) single crystals as a model system, we precisely resolve, for the first time, the incommensurately modulated structure of a conductive metal-organic framework at 100 K (modulation vector q = 0.39143(12) c*) via temperature-dependent single-crystal X-ray diffraction. The subsequent observation of a reversible metal-semiconductor transition around 350 K, which perfectly synchronizes with the disappearance of the structural modulation, provides convincing evidence for the electronic origin of the lattice distortion. Guest water molecules stabilize the modulated phase by synergistically regulating the relative rotation of the linkers and the interlayer spacing, thereby optimizing the inter-linker interactions. This work establishes a concrete experimental criterion for one-dimensional charge density wave in metal-organic frameworks and provides an ideal platform for probing coupled electronic-lattice modulations.

AI.tldr: First direct observation of incommensurate charge density wave (CDW) in conductive metal-organic framework (Pr3HHTP2) by single-crystal X-ray diffraction, revealing structural modulation at 100 K and its correlation with metal-semiconductor transition at 350 K.
AI.motivation: The intrinsic incommensurate charge density wave in MOFs has remained elusive due to lack of direct evidence linking atomic-scale structural modulation to macroscopic electronic properties.
AI.method: Temperature-dependent single-crystal X-ray diffraction on high-quality Pr3HHTP2 (HHTP = 2,3,6,7,10,11-hexahydroxytriphenylene) single crystals.
AI.result: Resolved incommensurately modulated structure at 100 K with modulation vector q = 0.39143(12) c*. Discovered reversible metal-semiconductor transition around 350 K that perfectly synchronizes with disappearance of structural modulation. Guest water molecules stabilize the modulated phase by regulating linker rotation and interlayer spacing.
AI.conclusion: Establishes experimental criterion for 1D CDW in MOFs and provides platform for probing coupled electronic-lattice modulations.

title: Intrinsic i-wave altermagnetism in 2D graphene superlattices
summary: Altermagnets feature unconventional magnetism due to their momentum-dependent spin splitting purely driven by magnetic order, for which a variety of transition-metal-based d-wave altermagnets have been proposed. However, carbon-based altermagnets in graphene structures remain elusive, even though magnetism in graphene nanostructures has been widely demonstrated. Here, we establish a symmetry-guided design principle to engineer i-wave altermagnets in graphene antidot superlattices and demonstrate the emergence of altermagnetic states in specific monolayer and bilayer graphene superlattices. By combining first principles methods and atomistic tight binding models, we show the appearance of an interaction-induced i-wave altermagnetic splitting, stemming from the intrinsic magnetic instability of 2D graphene antidot superlattices. Our work establishes a strategy to engineer i-wave altermagnetism in a graphene platform, putting forward a carbon-based platform for altermagnetic spintronics.
